channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Israel Bombs Beirut Outskirts as Fighting With Hezbollah Escalates
item.description: U.S. efforts for a truce in Lebanon appear to have stalled. Israel accused Hezbollah of firing at Israeli territory. Iran swiftly retaliated.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hassan Ammar/Associated Press
item.media:description: Rescue workers are seen on Sunday at a damaged building in a neighborhood on the southern outskirts of Beirut, a longtime Hezbollah stronghold.

item.title: Iranians Sink Into Despair Facing War Deaths and Skyrocketing Inflation
item.description: An imploding economy is causing hopelessness among both pro- and anti-government Iranians. And for those who wished for regime change, the letdown is palpable.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran in April. Daily life in Iran, already difficult, has grown harder in the months since the war began, with rampant inflation, civilian deaths and damaged infrastructure.

item.title: Scott Pelley on the Bari Weiss Era and His Last Days at ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: Lulu Garcia-Navarro sits down for an exclusive interview with the now-former CBS News correspondent.
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Scott Pelley’s Interview With The New York Times
item.description: Mr. Pelley, who was at CBS News for 37 years, including as a White House correspondent and a “60 Minutes” correspondent, spoke in his first extended interview since he was fired.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: In his first sit-down interview since he was fired from CBS News, Scott Pelley talks about his recent experiences at the network.

item.title: ‘60 Minutes’ Is a ‘Cage Full of Tigers.’ Can Nick Bilton Lead It?
item.description: The tech journalist and filmmaker was a surprise choice to lead a famously combative newsroom. His first week was chaos.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Sipa USA, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Nick Bilton, the executive producer of “60 Minutes.”

item.title: RFK Jr. Appears Disengaged on Many Health Department Matters Beyond Vaccines
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has demonstrated little interest in managing his sprawling department as he focuses on food and vaccine policies, according to colleagues.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Kennedy, who is isolated from much of his top staff, leans on a small number of key advisers.

item.title: Trump Defends Compensation Fund and Iran War in ‘Meet the Press’ Interview
item.description: In a lengthy interview with NBC’s “Meet the Press,” the president again vowed that gas prices would go down when the war in Iran ends.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump speaking to reporters on Air Force One on Friday.

item.title: Lawsuit Aims to Stop U.F.C. Fights at White House on Trump’s Birthday
item.description: A federal lawsuit said the event, set for June 14, was unlawfully planned and designed to benefit Mr. Trump and his allies.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: A 600-ton steel arch has risen on the South Lawn of the White House as a venue for the upcoming Ultimate Fighting Championship event.

item.title: Democrats in Congress Grapple With Concerns Over Platner Allegations
item.description: Representative Ro Khanna said that he believed the account of a woman who accused Graham Platner of physically threatening behavior, and urged his campaign not to criticize her.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner campaigning in Sabattus, Maine, in April.

item.title: Tony Awards 2026 Red Carpet: See the Looks of Broadway’s Biggest Stars
item.description: Broadway’s biggest names have started to arrive for the 79th Tony Awards. Here’s what they wore to walk the midnight blue carpet.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis/Reuters
item.media:description: Jeremy Pope, one of the show’s planned presenters, making an entrance that can only be described as “theatrical.”

item.title: Tony Award Winners 2026: The Updating List
item.description: The Tony Awards are underway at Radio City Music Hall in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The ceremony will be hosted by the pop singer-songwriter Pink.

item.title: June Squibb, the ‘Foulest Mouth on Broadway’, Is Up for a Tony at 96
item.description: At 96, June Squibb is one of the oldest acting nominees in the history of the Tony Awards. Back in the 1960s, she was known for an entirely different distinction.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: June Squibb has received her first Tony nomination, for her featured role in “Marjorie Prime.”

item.title: Knicks Watch Party at MSG Is Canceled as Game 3 Security Ramps Up for Trump
item.description: President Trump and Mayor Zohran Mamdani are expected to attend the game on Monday at Madison Square Garden. A viewing party on Friday led to more than a dozen arrests.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully and Maia Coleman
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: After a watch party for Game 2 of the N.B.A. finals led to violence, a Game 3 party outside Madison Square Garden was canceled.

item.title: Between the Knicks and the Pope, Villanova Finds the Spotlight
item.description: Villanova University alumni have been grabbing headlines, both on the court and in the Vatican.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Mark Bonamo
item.media:credit: Nathan Ray Seebeck/Imagn Images, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: New York Knicks guard Jalen Brunson and guard Josh Hart react after a play against the Orlando Magic last November.

item.title: They Have Yet to Sign a Lease. But They’re Furious Over $3,100 Rents.
item.description: Climate change. Gun violence. War. Generations of young people have organized over issues they fear threaten the future. Will housing be the next cause?
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson
item.media:credit: Roshni Khatri for The New York Times
item.media:description: Angelo Mazza, who lives with his family in Astoria, Queens, has watched an affordability crisis descend on New York, his hometown.

item.title: How Bank Tellers Are Stopping Customers From Giving Money to Scammers
item.description: With cybercrime continuing to rise, some banks are testing new prevention strategies. JPMorgan Chase hired a behavioral scientist.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Battista, who works at a Chase Bank in Scarsdale, N.Y., prevented a customer from transferring $9,000 into an unknown account.

item.title: How People Working in Debt Collection Handle Abuse From Callers
item.description: More Americans are taking on debt and falling behind on bills. The people calling them to collect often endure a torrent of verbal abuse and threats of violence.
item.dc:creator: Kali Bomeli
item.media:credit: Whitten Sabbatini for The New York Times
item.media:description: Guybrielle Madison has worked as a debt collector for six years. She speaks to about 50 people each day, and being called racial slurs is common.

item.title: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle CEO, Confessed to Fraud. The Board Let Her Stay in Charge.
item.description: The final months of the fashion tech start-up CaaStle included fake audits, stock buybacks and a damage control strategy led by the chief executive who defrauded it.
item.dc:creator: Anita Raghavan
item.media:credit: Larry Neumeister/Associated Press
item.media:description: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle’s co-founder, pleaded guilty to securities fraud in March.

item.title: Seattle’s Incredibly Loud Street Preachers Eagerly Await the World Cup
item.description: Long the bane of Mariners and Seahawks fans, evangelical ministers with large speakers are preparing for the “incredible opportunity” of screaming the word of God at 750,000 soccer fans.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ron Cardiel, a preacher, calls it a “no-escape mentality to hearing the word of God,” evangelizing through loudspeakers to captive audiences on lines in Seattle.

item.title: Robert Coles, Pulitzer-Winning Child Psychiatrist, Is Dead at 97
item.description: His five-volume “Children of Crisis” series, published between 1967 and 1977, drew on his conversations with American children whose voices were not often heard.
item.dc:creator: Douglas Martin
item.media:credit: Jodi Hilton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Coles eschewed ideologies and psychiatric orthodoxies to visit the homes of children — first in the American South and then around the world — to listen intently to what they had to say.

item.title: For Disabled Travelers in Search of Adventure, Adrenaline-Fueled Options Are Growing
item.description: For disabled travelers in search of adventure, there are a growing number of adrenaline-fueled options. In Oregon, one wheelchair user went tree-climbing.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao and Michael Hanson
item.media:description: Mr. Babb with Ramon Moreno, one of the workers assisting with his climb.

item.title: You Can Walk to the World Cup in New Jersey. But Should You?
item.description: We decided to make the trek and find out.
item.dc:creator: Alex Wolfe and Thomas Wilson

item.title: Who Will Account for Another Foreign Policy Failure?
item.description: The American foreign policy class should shed its addiction to military force.
item.dc:creator: Peter Beinart
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
American Enterprise Institute for Public Policy Research

item.title: On Patrol With Tokyo’s New Litter Police
item.description: Visitor numbers to Shibuya are surging and trash cans are scarce. Enter the city patrol.
item.dc:creator: Kiuko Notoya
item.media:credit: Kiuko Notoya/The New York Times
item.media:description: On the streets of Shibuya, patrol teams caught 10 people littering on the first day of the new ordinance, 15 on the second day and 9 on the third, according to city officials.
